note: Premade, hand-checked high-yield MCAT flashcards, keyed by full topic tag (must match ids in mcat_outline.json). This is the deck the app auto-loads on first run so content is ALWAYS ready and the student never has to import anything. Facts are curated across every topic in the AAMC outline; phrasing is original. Every listed topic ships with real cards (no synthetic padding).

mcat::bio_biochem::amino_acids: Which amino acid is achiral?
Glycine (its side chain is a single hydrogen).
What is the net charge of an amino acid at its isoelectric point (pI)?
Zero (it exists as a zwitterion).
Which three amino acids are basic (positively charged at physiological pH)?
Lysine, arginine, and histidine.
Which two amino acids are acidic (negatively charged at physiological pH)?
Aspartate and glutamate.
Which amino acid forms disulfide bonds?
Cysteine (through its thiol side chain).
Which amino acids are aromatic?
Phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan.
How is a peptide bond formed?
By a condensation (dehydration) reaction that releases water.
At a pH below its pI, what is the net charge of an amino acid?
Positive (pH < pI gives net positive charge).
Which amino acid has a secondary amine and introduces kinks in a chain?
Proline.
When pH equals a side chain's pKa, what is its protonation state?
50% protonated and 50% deprotonated.
mcat::bio_biochem::protein_structure: What stabilizes the alpha helix?
Hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl (C=O) and amide (N-H) groups.
What level of protein structure are disulfide bonds associated with?
Tertiary (and quaternary) structure.
What is primary structure?
The linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds.
What are the two main types of secondary structure?
Alpha helices and beta pleated sheets.
What defines quaternary structure?
The assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into one functional protein.
What force primarily drives a globular protein to fold in water?
The hydrophobic effect (nonpolar residues cluster inside).
What is denaturation?
Loss of a protein's 3-D shape and function without breaking peptide bonds.
Sickle cell anemia results from what change in primary structure?
A single glutamate-to-valine substitution in beta-globin.
What holds adjacent strands of a beta-pleated sheet together?
Backbone hydrogen bonds between the strands.
Why is the peptide bond planar?
It has partial double-bond character from resonance.
mcat::bio_biochem::enzymes: What does a competitive inhibitor do to Km and Vmax?
Increases apparent Km; Vmax unchanged.
What does a noncompetitive inhibitor do to Km and Vmax?
Km unchanged; Vmax decreases.
How do enzymes speed up reactions?
By lowering activation energy; they do not change delta G or the equilibrium.
What is Km in Michaelis-Menten kinetics?
The substrate concentration at which the rate is half of Vmax.
What does a low Km indicate about enzyme-substrate affinity?
High affinity.
What does an uncompetitive inhibitor do to Km and Vmax?
Decreases both Km and Vmax.
What is feedback inhibition?
When a pathway's end product inhibits an upstream enzyme.
Where does a competitive inhibitor bind?
The active site.
What are zymogens?
Inactive enzyme precursors activated by cleavage (e.g., trypsinogen to trypsin).
Cofactor vs coenzyme: which is organic?
Coenzymes are organic (often vitamin-derived); cofactors are inorganic ions.
mcat::bio_biochem::carbohydrates: What is the general empirical formula of a carbohydrate?
(CH2O)n.
What glycosidic bond links glucose in cellulose?
Beta-1,4.
What glycosidic bonds are found in glycogen?
Alpha-1,4 (linear) with alpha-1,6 branches.
Which sugar stereochemistry (D or L) dominates in biology?
D-sugars.
Aldose vs ketose: what is the difference?
An aldose has a terminal aldehyde; a ketose has an internal ketone.
What are anomers?
Stereoisomers differing at the anomeric carbon (alpha vs beta).
What is a reducing sugar?
A sugar with a free anomeric carbon that can be oxidized.
Glucose + fructose forms which disaccharide?
Sucrose.
What are epimers?
Diastereomers differing at a single chiral center (e.g., glucose vs galactose).
What is the storage polysaccharide in animals?
Glycogen (in liver and muscle).
mcat::bio_biochem::lipids: What makes a fatty acid saturated?
It has no carbon-carbon double bonds.
Why do unsaturated fats have lower melting points?
Cis double bonds kink the chains and prevent tight packing.
What is the structure of a triacylglycerol?
Glycerol esterified to three fatty acids.
What property of phospholipids drives membrane formation?
They are amphipathic (polar head, nonpolar tails).
What is the precursor of all steroid hormones?
Cholesterol.
What does cholesterol do in a membrane?
It buffers membrane fluidity against temperature changes.
What are the fat-soluble vitamins?
A, D, E, and K.
What bond links a fatty acid to glycerol?
An ester bond.
What are the products of triacylglycerol saponification?
Glycerol and fatty acid salts (soap).
Which lipoprotein is the 'good cholesterol'?
HDL (returns cholesterol to the liver).
mcat::bio_biochem::nucleic_acids: Which bonds hold complementary DNA strands together?
Hydrogen bonds between base pairs.
Purines vs pyrimidines: which are double-ringed?
Purines (adenine, guanine).
How many hydrogen bonds form between G and C?
Three.
How many hydrogen bonds form between A and T?
Two.
What sugar is in DNA vs RNA?
Deoxyribose in DNA; ribose in RNA.
In which direction is a nucleic acid strand synthesized?
Which base replaces thymine in RNA?
Uracil.
What are the pyrimidines in DNA?
Cytosine and thymine.
What bond joins nucleotides within a strand?
Phosphodiester bonds.
What does Chargaff's rule state?
In DNA, %A = %T and %G = %C.
mcat::bio_biochem::dna_replication: In which direction does DNA polymerase synthesize?
5' to 3' (reading the template 3' to 5').
Why is the lagging strand made in Okazaki fragments?
It runs antiparallel, so it is synthesized discontinuously.
What enzyme unwinds the double helix?
Helicase.
What enzyme joins Okazaki fragments?
DNA ligase.
What provides the 3'-OH primer for DNA polymerase?
An RNA primer made by primase.
Is DNA replication conservative or semiconservative?
Semiconservative (each daughter has one old and one new strand).
What relieves supercoiling ahead of the fork?
Topoisomerase (e.g., DNA gyrase).
What proofreading activity does DNA polymerase have?
3' to 5' exonuclease activity.
What enzyme extends the ends of linear chromosomes?
Telomerase.
Leading vs lagging strand: which is continuous?
The leading strand.
mcat::bio_biochem::transcription_transla: What enzyme carries out transcription?
RNA polymerase.
Where does eukaryotic transcription occur?
In the nucleus.
What three modifications process a eukaryotic pre-mRNA?
5' cap, poly-A tail, and intron splicing.
What is a codon?
Three mRNA nucleotides coding for one amino acid.
What is the start codon, and which amino acid does it code for?
AUG; methionine.
What are the three stop codons?
UAA, UAG, and UGA.
What molecule brings amino acids to the ribosome?
tRNA (matched by its anticodon).
What does it mean that the genetic code is degenerate?
Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid.
Introns vs exons: which are expressed?
Exons (introns are spliced out).
In which direction is mRNA read during translation?
mcat::bio_biochem::gene_regulation: What is an operon?
A cluster of genes under one promoter, common in prokaryotes.
Is the lac operon inducible or repressible?
Inducible (turned on by lactose/allolactose).
Is the trp operon inducible or repressible?
Repressible (tryptophan acts as a corepressor).
What do enhancers do?
Increase transcription of a gene, even from a distance.
How does DNA methylation usually affect expression?
It generally silences (represses) transcription.
How does histone acetylation affect transcription?
It loosens chromatin and increases transcription.
What is euchromatin?
Loosely packed, transcriptionally active chromatin.
What are transcription factors?
Proteins that bind DNA to regulate transcription.
What do microRNAs (miRNAs) do?
Bind mRNA to block translation or trigger its degradation.
What does the lac repressor do when lactose is absent?
It binds the operator and blocks transcription.
mcat::bio_biochem::glycolysis: Net ATP yield of glycolysis per glucose?
What are the products of glycolysis per glucose?
What is the fate of pyruvate anaerobically in humans?
Which step is the first committed, irreversible step?
Direct transfer of a phosphate to ADP to make ATP (no ETC needed).
What enzyme traps glucose in the cell in step 1?
mcat::bio_biochem::citric_acid_cycle: Where does the citric acid cycle occur in eukaryotes?
The mitochondrial matrix.
What molecule combines with oxaloacetate to enter the cycle?
Acetyl-CoA.
Per turn, how many NADH, FADH2, and GTP are produced?
3 NADH, 1 FADH2, and 1 GTP (plus 2 CO2).
What is the rate-limiting enzyme of the cycle?
Isocitrate dehydrogenase.
How many turns of the cycle occur per glucose?
Two (one per pyruvate).
What converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA?
The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.
What is the main purpose of the cycle?
To produce NADH and FADH2 for the electron transport chain.
What molecule is regenerated at the end of each turn?
Oxaloacetate.
How many CO2 molecules are released per turn?
Two.
What indirectly makes the cycle require oxygen?
It needs the ETC to regenerate NAD+ and FAD.
mcat::bio_biochem::oxidative_phosphoryla: Final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain?
Molecular oxygen (O2).
What does the proton-motive force drive?
ATP synthesis via ATP synthase.
Where is the electron transport chain located?
The inner mitochondrial membrane.
About how many ATP result per glucose from aerobic respiration?
About 30-32 ATP.
What do NADH and FADH2 donate to the chain?
High-energy electrons.
Where do protons accumulate to build the gradient?
The intermembrane space.
What is chemiosmosis?
ATP synthesis powered by protons flowing down their gradient through ATP synthase.
What happens to the ETC without oxygen?
It stalls; NADH cannot be reoxidized and aerobic ATP production halts.
Which yields more ATP: NADH or FADH2?
NADH (it enters the chain earlier).
What is the role of ATP synthase?
It uses the proton gradient to phosphorylate ADP to ATP.
mcat::bio_biochem::cell_biology: What is the fluid mosaic model?
A fluid phospholipid bilayer with embedded, mobile proteins.
What organelle is the site of protein synthesis?
The ribosome.
What does the rough endoplasmic reticulum do?
Synthesizes and modifies secreted and membrane proteins.
What is the function of the Golgi apparatus?
Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for secretion.
What do lysosomes contain?
Hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes active at low pH.
What distinguishes prokaryotes from eukaryotes?
Prokaryotes lack a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles.
What transport moves solutes against a gradient using ATP?
Active transport.
What does the sodium-potassium pump do per ATP?
Pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in.
What happens to a cell placed in a hypertonic solution?
Water leaves and the cell shrinks (crenation).
Which cytoskeletal element forms the mitotic spindle?
Microtubules (made of tubulin).
mcat::chem_phys::atomic_structure: What do the quantum numbers n, l, ml, and ms describe?
Shell, subshell shape, orbital orientation, and electron spin.
What is the maximum number of electrons in one orbital?
Two, with opposite spins.
What is Hund's rule?
Electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing.
What is the Pauli exclusion principle?
No two electrons in an atom share all four quantum numbers.
What is an isotope?
Atoms of an element with the same protons but different neutrons.
What defines the atomic number?
The number of protons.
What is the Aufbau principle?
Electrons fill the lowest-energy orbitals first.
How many electrons can a p subshell hold?
Six.
What is the ground state of an atom?
Its lowest-energy electron configuration.
What happens when an excited electron falls to a lower level?
It emits a photon of energy E = hf.
mcat::chem_phys::periodic_trends: How does atomic radius change left to right across a period?
It decreases.
How does ionization energy change down a group?
It decreases.
How does electronegativity change across a period?
It increases.
Which element is the most electronegative?
Fluorine.
Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
More electron shells are added.
How does electron affinity trend across a period?
It generally becomes more negative (more energy released).
Which group has the highest ionization energies?
The noble gases.
What is effective nuclear charge?
The net positive charge felt by valence electrons.
How does metallic character change down a group?
It increases.
Cation vs neutral atom: which is smaller?
The cation.
mcat::chem_phys::bonding: Ionic vs covalent bond: what is the difference?
Ionic transfers electrons; covalent shares them.
What is a polar covalent bond?
A covalent bond with unequal electron sharing.
VSEPR: what shape has 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs?
Tetrahedral (about 109.5 degrees).
What geometry results from 3 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair?
Trigonal pyramidal.
What is the hybridization of a double-bonded carbon?
sp2.
What is the strongest noncovalent intermolecular force?
Hydrogen bonding.
What holds nonpolar molecules together?
London dispersion forces.
How many electrons are shared in a double bond?
Four (two pairs).
What is a coordinate covalent bond?
A covalent bond where both electrons come from one atom.
Sigma vs pi bond: which forms by head-on overlap?
The sigma bond (pi bonds overlap side-on).
mcat::chem_phys::stoichiometry: What is Avogadro's number?
6.022 x 10^23 particles per mole.
What is the limiting reagent?
The reactant consumed first, which caps the product yield.
What is percent yield?
(actual yield / theoretical yield) x 100%.
What is an empirical formula?
The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
How do you find moles from mass?
Divide the mass by the molar mass.
What do coefficients in a balanced equation represent?
Mole ratios of reactants and products.
What is molarity?
Moles of solute per liter of solution.
What is conserved in a balanced chemical equation?
Mass and atoms (conservation of mass).
What is the molar mass of water?
About 18 g/mol.
If limiting reagent moles double (other in excess), what happens to product?
It doubles.
mcat::chem_phys::thermodynamics: Sign of delta G for a spontaneous process?
Negative.
Relationship between delta G, delta H, and delta S?
delta G = delta H - T*delta S.
What is the first law of thermodynamics?
Energy is conserved; delta U = Q - W.
What does a positive delta H indicate?
An endothermic reaction (absorbs heat).
What does entropy (S) measure?
The disorder or number of microstates of a system.
Relationship between delta G-standard and K?
delta G-standard = -RT*ln(K).
When is a reaction spontaneous at all temperatures?
When delta H < 0 and delta S > 0.
What is a state function?
A property that depends only on state, not path (e.g., enthalpy).
What does q = m*c*delta T calculate?
Heat transferred from mass, specific heat, and temperature change.
What is delta G at equilibrium?
Zero.
mcat::chem_phys::kinetics: What does the rate-determining step control?
The overall reaction rate (it is the slowest step).
How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
By lowering the activation energy via a new pathway.
What does the Arrhenius equation relate?
The rate constant to temperature and activation energy.
For a first-order reaction, what does the rate depend on?
One reactant's concentration to the first power.
How does raising temperature affect reaction rate?
It increases the rate (more molecules exceed Ea).
What is activation energy?
The minimum energy needed for reactants to react.
Does a catalyst change delta G or Keq?
No; it only speeds reaching equilibrium.
What is the order of a reaction?
The sum of the exponents in the rate law.
How does a catalyst affect the reverse reaction?
It speeds the reverse reaction equally.
What is the transition state?
The highest-energy, unstable arrangement at the top of the barrier.
mcat::chem_phys::equilibrium: What does Le Chatelier's principle predict?
A system shifts to counteract an applied stress.
Adding product shifts equilibrium which way?
Toward reactants (left).
What does a large Keq indicate?
Products are favored at equilibrium.
How does a catalyst affect equilibrium position?
It does not; it only speeds reaching it.
Increasing pressure shifts gas equilibrium toward which side?
The side with fewer moles of gas.
What is the reaction quotient Q used for?
Comparing to K to predict the direction of shift.
If Q < K, which way does the reaction proceed?
Forward, toward products.
What is Ksp?
The solubility product constant of a sparingly soluble salt.
What is the common ion effect?
An added common ion decreases a salt's solubility.
How does heating shift an endothermic reaction?
Toward products (heat acts as a reactant).
mcat::chem_phys::acids_bases: What is the pH of a 0.01 M strong acid solution?
At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, pH equals what?
What is the relationship between pH and pOH?
A weak acid together with its conjugate base.
Strong vs weak acid: which fully dissociates?
pH at the equivalence point of a strong acid-strong base titration?
mcat::chem_phys::kinematics: Acceleration due to gravity near Earth's surface?
About 9.8 m/s^2 downward.
For a projectile, what is the horizontal acceleration (ignoring air)?
Zero.
Equation for velocity under constant acceleration?
v = v0 + a*t.
Equation for displacement under constant acceleration?
At the top of a projectile's arc, what is its vertical velocity?
Zero.
Are horizontal and vertical motions independent in projectiles?
Yes.
Speed vs velocity: what is the difference?
Velocity is a vector (has direction); speed is scalar.
What is the acceleration of an object in free fall?
g (about 9.8 m/s^2) downward.
What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?
Acceleration.
What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
Displacement.
mcat::chem_phys::force_energy: State Newton's second law.
F = m*a (net force equals mass times acceleration).
What is the work-energy theorem?
Net work done equals the change in kinetic energy.
What is the formula for kinetic energy?
What is gravitational potential energy near Earth?
PE = m*g*h.
What is Newton's third law?
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
What is the work done by a force?
W = F*d*cos(theta).
What is power?
The rate of doing work (P = W/t = F*v).
What is conserved without nonconservative forces?
Mechanical energy (KE + PE).
What is the maximum static friction force?
f = mu_s * N.
Centripetal acceleration points in what direction?
Toward the center of the circular path.
mcat::chem_phys::fluids: What is the continuity equation for fluids?
A1*v1 = A2*v2 (flow rate is conserved).
What does Bernoulli's principle say about fast-moving fluid?
It has lower pressure.
What is the buoyant force (Archimedes' principle)?
Equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
What is hydrostatic pressure at depth?
P = rho*g*h (plus any surface pressure).
What is Pascal's principle?
Pressure on a confined fluid is transmitted equally throughout.
When does an object float?
When its average density is less than the fluid's.
What is specific gravity?
A substance's density relative to water.
In a narrower pipe section, how does fluid speed change?
It increases (by continuity).
What are the SI units of pressure?
Pascals (N/m^2).
As fluid velocity increases, what happens to pressure (Bernoulli)?
Pressure decreases.
mcat::chem_phys::circuits: In a series circuit, what is the same through each element?
In a parallel circuit, what is the same across each branch?
How do resistances add in series?
How do resistances combine in parallel?
Charge and energy in an electric field.
Current in equals current out (charge conservation).
Voltage changes around a loop sum to zero (energy conservation).
Adding resistors in parallel does what to total resistance?
mcat::psych_soc::sensation_perception: What is the absolute threshold?
The minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.
Which theory explains color vision via three cone types?
Trichromatic (Young-Helmholtz) theory.
Sensation vs perception: what is the difference?
Sensation detects stimuli; perception interprets them.
What is Weber's law?
The just-noticeable difference is a constant proportion of the stimulus.
What is transduction?
Converting physical stimulus energy into neural signals.
What does signal detection theory add?
It factors in noise and decision bias when detecting stimuli.
What are the two retinal photoreceptors?
Rods (dim light) and cones (color and detail).
What is bottom-up processing?
Perception built from raw sensory input.
What is top-down processing?
Perception guided by expectations and prior knowledge.
What is sensory adaptation?
Reduced sensitivity to a constant, unchanging stimulus.
mcat::psych_soc::cognition: What are Piaget's four stages of cognitive development?
Sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, formal operational.
What is assimilation (Piaget)?
Fitting new information into an existing schema.
What is accommodation (Piaget)?
Changing a schema to fit new information.
Algorithm vs heuristic: what is the difference?
An algorithm guarantees a solution; a heuristic is a fast shortcut.
What is confirmation bias?
Favoring information that confirms existing beliefs.
What is functional fixedness?
Only seeing an object used in its usual way.
What is the availability heuristic?
Judging likelihood by how easily examples come to mind.
What is the representativeness heuristic?
Judging probability by similarity to a prototype.
Fluid vs crystallized intelligence?
Fluid solves novel problems; crystallized is accumulated knowledge.
What is a mental set?
A tendency to approach problems with a previously successful strategy.
mcat::psych_soc::memory: Which brain structure is critical for forming new long-term memories?
The hippocampus.
What is proactive interference?
Old information disrupts learning of new information.
What is retroactive interference?
New information disrupts recall of old information.
What are the three stages of the Atkinson-Shiffrin model?
Sensory memory, short-term memory, long-term memory.
Explicit vs implicit memory: what is the difference?
Explicit is conscious (facts/events); implicit is unconscious (skills).
What is the spacing effect?
Better retention when study is spread out rather than crammed.
What are the two subtypes of explicit memory?
Semantic (facts) and episodic (personal events).
What is the serial position effect?
Better recall of the first (primacy) and last (recency) list items.
What does the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve describe?
The exponential decline of retention over time.
What is chunking?
Grouping items into meaningful units to expand short-term capacity.
mcat::psych_soc::learning: In classical conditioning, what is the CS before conditioning?
A neutral stimulus.
Which reinforcement schedule is most resistant to extinction?
Variable ratio.
What is classical conditioning?
Learning to associate a neutral stimulus with a reflex-eliciting one.
In Pavlov's experiment, what is the unconditioned stimulus?
The food (it naturally elicits salivation).
What is operant conditioning?
Learning driven by consequences (reinforcement and punishment).
What is negative reinforcement?
Removing an aversive stimulus to increase a behavior.
Negative reinforcement vs punishment: what is the difference?
Reinforcement increases behavior; punishment decreases it.
What is observational learning?
Learning by watching and imitating others (Bandura's Bobo doll).
What is shaping?
Reinforcing successive approximations toward a target behavior.
What is latent learning?
Learning that appears only once there is an incentive to show it.
mcat::psych_soc::motivation_emotion: What is the James-Lange theory of emotion?
Emotion follows the body's physiological response.
What is the Cannon-Bard theory?
Arousal and emotion occur simultaneously and independently.
What is the Schachter-Singer (two-factor) theory?
Emotion = physiological arousal + a cognitive label.
What is the Yerkes-Dodson law?
Performance peaks at moderate arousal.
What is at the base of Maslow's hierarchy?
Physiological needs.
What is drive-reduction theory?
Behavior reduces internal tension to restore homeostasis.
What is intrinsic motivation?
Doing an activity for its own inherent satisfaction.
What brain structure is central to fear?
The amygdala.
What is the overjustification effect?
External rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation.
What are Ekman's universal basic emotions?
Happiness, sadness, fear, disgust, anger, and surprise.
mcat::psych_soc::identity: What is self-concept?
The sum of one's beliefs about oneself.
What do Erikson's psychosocial stages describe?
A conflict resolved at each life stage (e.g., identity vs role confusion).
What is the looking-glass self (Cooley)?
Forming self-image from how we think others see us.
What is Freud's structure of personality?
The id, ego, and superego.
What is self-efficacy (Bandura)?
One's belief in their ability to succeed at a task.
Ideal self vs real self (Rogers): what is the effect of a large gap?
A large gap lowers self-worth (incongruence).
What are Kohlberg's three levels of moral development?
Preconventional, conventional, and postconventional.
What defines identity status in Marcia's theory?
The presence of crisis (exploration) and commitment.
What is a reference group?
A group used as a standard for self-evaluation.
Role strain vs role conflict: what is role strain?
Tension among the demands within a single role.
mcat::psych_soc::social_thinking: What is the fundamental attribution error?
Overattributing others' behavior to disposition over situation.
What is the actor-observer bias?
Attributing our own actions to situation but others' to disposition.
What is cognitive dissonance?
Discomfort from conflicting attitudes/behaviors that motivates change.
Conformity vs compliance vs obedience: what drives each?
Group norms, a request, and an authority's order, respectively.
What did the Milgram experiment show?
People obey authority even to harm others.
What is the bystander effect?
The presence of others lowers the chance any one person helps.
What is diffusion of responsibility?
Feeling less personal duty to act when others are present.
Stereotype vs prejudice vs discrimination?
A belief, an attitude, and a behavior, respectively.
What is the self-serving bias?
Crediting successes to oneself and blaming failures on outside factors.
What is social facilitation?
Better performance on well-learned tasks when others are present.
mcat::psych_soc::social_structure: What are the three major sociological perspectives?
Functionalism, conflict theory, and symbolic interactionism.
What does functionalism emphasize?
How social structures work together to maintain stability.
What does conflict theory emphasize?
Competition over limited resources and power inequality.
What is the focus of symbolic interactionism?
How people create meaning through everyday interactions.
What is social stratification?
The hierarchical ranking of people into social classes.
Master status vs ascribed status: what is a master status?
The status that most dominates a person's social identity.
What is cultural capital?
Non-financial assets (education, tastes) that aid social mobility.
Folkway vs more: what is the difference?
Folkways are casual norms; mores are morally significant norms.
What is anomie (Durkheim)?
A state of normlessness during rapid social change.
What is a social institution?
An established structure (family, education) meeting a societal need.
mcat::psych_soc::demographics: What is the demographic transition model?
The shift from high to low birth and death rates as a society industrializes.
Push vs pull factors in migration?
Push factors drive people away; pull factors attract them.
What is the fertility rate?
The average number of children born per woman.
What is social mobility?
Movement of individuals or groups between social positions.
Intragenerational vs intergenerational mobility?
Within one lifetime vs across generations.
What is urbanization?
The rising share of a population living in cities.
What is a population pyramid?
A graph of a population's age and sex distribution.
What three factors change a population's size?
Births, deaths, and migration.
What is globalization?
The growing interconnection of economies and cultures worldwide.
What causes demographic aging of a population?
Lower fertility and longer life expectancy raising the median age.
mcat::psych_soc::biological_behavior: Which division of the nervous system drives 'fight or flight'?
The sympathetic nervous system.
What does the parasympathetic nervous system do?
Promotes 'rest and digest' functions.
Which neurotransmitter is tied to reward and movement?
Dopamine.
What part of a neuron receives incoming signals?
The dendrites.
What is the resting membrane potential of a typical neuron?
About -70 mV.
Which ion's influx triggers the action potential?
Sodium (Na+).
Which brain region regulates hunger, thirst, and temperature?
The hypothalamus.
What is the 'master gland' of the endocrine system?
The pituitary gland.
What is the function of myelin?
It insulates axons and speeds action potential conduction.
Which lobe contains the primary visual cortex?
The occipital lobe.
mcat::cars: What does CARS test?
Reading comprehension and reasoning using ONLY the passage (no outside knowledge).
Should you use outside knowledge on CARS questions?
No; answers must be supported by the passage itself.
What should you pin down about a CARS passage first?
The author's main idea, argument, and tone.
How should you treat extreme words like 'always' or 'never' in choices?
Be cautious; they are often too strong to be supported.
Inference vs detail question: what is the difference?
Inference requires reasoning beyond the text; detail is stated directly.
Why track the author's tone or attitude?
Questions often hinge on whether the author agrees or disagrees.
What is the key to strengthen/weaken questions?
Pick the choice that most directly supports or undermines the argument.
How do you handle an unfamiliar CARS topic?
Focus on the passage's structure and logic, not prior familiarity.
Why is process of elimination central to CARS?
The right answer is the best supported; eliminate unsupported choices.
What is the danger of a 'half-right' answer choice?
One wrong word makes the whole choice wrong; read every part.
